segments.inferno-28-g86: Short spoken-preface introducing direct speech: E (and) links this utterance to the prior action; io is the stressed subject pronoun (“I”) placed before the indirect object; a lui marks the indirect object (“to him”) using the preposition a + disjunctive pronoun lui. The colon signals that what follows is the speaker’s quoted words.
segments.inferno-28-g87: Two coordinated imperatives addressed to the interlocutor. Dimostrami is the 2nd‑person singular imperative of dimostrare with the enclitic object pronoun -mi attached (show/point out to me). e joins the commands. dichiara is likewise a 2nd‑person singular imperative (tell/reveal); the subject tu is omitted but understood.
segments.inferno-28-g88: A conditional subordinate clause introduced by se (“if” — note: the provided sense list did not include this meaning, so -1 is used). vuo’ is the archaic/poetic contraction of vuoi (2nd‑person singular present “you want”). ch’ is the clitic form of che linking to the subjunctive clause. i (written i’ here) = io, the subject pronoun explicitly inserted before the verb (che i’ porti = che io porti); porti is present subjunctive 1st‑person singular of portare (used after se... che to express a wished/contingent action: “that I bring”). sù is an adverb “up”/“back” and di te is a prepositional phrase “about you/from you”; novella is a noun “news/report.”
segments.inferno-28-g89: A free interrogative noun phrase functioning as the content of the previous verb (dichiarare…): chi è colui = “who is that one?” Colui is the demonstrative pronoun “that one” and è is the 3rd‑person singular present of essere. da la is the preposition + article (poetically separated instead of dalla) meaning “from/of/with” in this context; veduta is a noun “appearance/view/sight” modified by the adjective amara (“bitter”), together yielding “the one with the bitter appearance” or “the one of the bitter sight.”
segments.inferno-28-g90: Allor 'then/thereupon' is a temporal connector. Puose is passato remoto of porre 'he placed/put'. La mano a la mascella: la mano is direct object 'the hand', a + la mascella marks the target 'to/on the jaw'. D’un suo compagno = contraction di + un 'of a companion of his'. E la bocca li aperse: la bocca is 'the mouth' and li is the dative/indirect object 'to him' (Dante frequently uses li for 'to him'); aperse is passato remoto 'opened'. The phrase describes a physical action: he put his hand on the companion's jaw and opened his mouth for him.
segments.inferno-28-g91: Gridando is the present participle (gerund) of gridare 'shouting/crying out', describing how the preceding action was accompanied.
segments.inferno-28-g92: Questi is a demonstrative pronoun 'this one/this man' used as subject. È is the present indicative of essere 'is'. Desso is an emphatic demonstrative 'he himself/the very one' (desso = stesso in older usage). The clause is a short identification: 'This is the very man.'
segments.inferno-28-g93: E non favella is a terse prohibition; e = 'and', non = 'do not'. Favella here is an archaic negative imperative or subordinated prohibitive form from favellare 'to speak' — in context it means 'do not speak' / 'let him not speak'. Dante sometimes uses older forms where the negative is placed before a bare verb form.
segments.inferno-28-g94: 'Questi' is a demonstrative pronoun (archaic/poetic form of 'questo') used as the subject 'this one' or 'he'; the comma after it sets up the following participial phrase that describes his condition.
segments.inferno-28-g95: 'scacciato' is a past participle (from 'scacciare') used adjectivally/participially ('having been driven away' or 'driven out') and modifies 'Questi', giving his state when the action occurs.
segments.inferno-28-g96: The verb 'sommerse' is a literary/past form (passato remoto) meaning 'overwhelmed/drowned' with the omitted subject 'Questi'; 'il dubitar' is an infinitive nominalized by the article ('the doubt') functioning as direct object, and 'in Cesare' indicates the target/place ('in Caesar's mind' or 'as regards Caesar').
segments.inferno-28-g97: 'affermando' is a gerund/participle meaning 'asserting', introducing the reported content; 'che' is the conjunction 'that' beginning the subordinate clause. '’l fornito' is the article + past participle used substantively ('the provided/supplied one' or 'the man who was furnished/equipped'), 'sempre' is 'always', 'con danno' 'to his/one's harm', 'l’attender' is an article + infinitive nominalizing 'waiting/expectation', and 'sofferse' (passato remoto of 'soffrire') is 'suffered/underwent' — together: 'asserting that the provided one always suffered harm in waiting.'
segments.inferno-28-g98: 'Oh quanto' is an exclamatory 'O how'; 'mi pareva' is the imperfect of 'parere' ('seemed to me'), with 'mi' marking the experiencer. 'sbigottito' is a past participle used adjectivally ('dismayed/terrified'). The prepositional phrase 'con la lingua tagliata ne la strozza' ('with the tongue cut in the throat') uses 'tagliata' (past participle) to describe the state and 'ne' is an archaic form of 'in' placed before 'la strozza' ('the throat'). 'Curïo' names the person described (apposition or nominative after the descriptive phrase).
segments.inferno-28-g99: 'ch’' is the clitic form of 'che' introducing a relative/explanatory clause; 'a dir' is an infinitive phrase meaning 'in saying' or 'to say' (here 'when he spoke'), composed of the preposition 'a' + infinitive 'dir' (poetic form of 'dire'). 'fu' is passato remoto of 'essere' ('was'), and 'così ardito' is the adjectival predicate 'so bold/daring' describing Curio's manner of speaking.
segments.inferno-28-g151: Così is an adverb meaning 'thus' and sets the manner. S’ is the clitic si elided before the vowel of osserva; here si + osserva (3rd-person singular present indicative) forms an impersonal/passive-like construction meaning 'is observed' or 'is fulfilled'. In me is a prepositional phrase ('in me') placed after the verb for emphasis, indicating the location where the contrapasso appears. Lo is the masculine singular definite article (a poetic/metrical choice instead of il) that introduces the noun contrapasso, which names the principle of fitting, poetic retribution.
segments.inferno-28-g21: Fronting the prepositional phrase 'Tra le gambe' places the location first; the verb 'pendevan' is the imperfect (3rd plural) 'pendevano' in a contracted/poetic form and agrees with the plural subject 'le minugia' (the clitic article 'le' + noun). 'Minugia' is a collective/visceral plural noun meaning entrails, literally 'guts' hanging down.
segments.inferno-28-g22: 'La corata pareva' uses the imperfect 'pareva' (seemed/appeared) with 'la corata' (the offal/viscera) as grammatical subject; Dante then links in apposition the phrase 'e ’l tristo sacco' (and the wretched sack). The relative clause 'che merda fa di quel che si trangugia' means 'that makes (into) shit of whatever is swallowed' — 'fa di' = 'makes of/turns into' and 'si trangugia' is a reflexive/impersonal construction meaning 'is swallowed' or 'one swallows'. The verb-noun combination 'merda fa' conveys the crude result: it 'makes [it] shit.'
segments.inferno-28-g23: 'Mentre che' = 'while'; 'tutto in lui' means 'everything about him' (tutto = everything) and is the object of the action. The phrase 'veder m’attacco' is a poetic word order: the infinitive 'veder' precedes the finite verb 'm'attacco' (mi attacco, 'I set myself to'/'I apply myself to'), where 'm’' is the clitic for 'mi' attached to the verb. So the sense is 'while I set myself to see everything about him.'
segments.inferno-28-g24: 'Guardommi' is an enclitic/past form meaning 'he looked at me' (guardò + mi compressed into one word); 'e con le man s’aperse il petto' describes a simultaneous action: 'and with his hands he opened his chest' — 's’aperse' is reflexive/passive-perfective 'opened himself' (he opened his own breast), and 'con le man' marks the instrumental means (with his hands). Word order puts the object 'il petto' after the verb for emphasis.
segments.inferno-28-g25: 'Dicendo' is the gerund 'saying,' indicating that what follows is reported speech and that the action of speaking is simultaneous with the preceding action.
segments.inferno-28-g26: Direct speech: 'Or vedi' = imperative 'Now see'; 'com’ io mi dilacco' = 'how I tear myself apart' with 'com’' = 'come' (how), 'io' explicit subject for emphasis, 'mi' reflexive pronoun, and 'dilacco' first-person present 'I tear apart/tear myself' (dilacerare-like).
segments.inferno-28-g27: Imperative 'vedi' ('see!') addresses the listener/reader; 'come' = 'how'; 'storpiato' is a past participle used adjectivally ('mutilated/maimed'); the verb 'è' follows the adjective (inverted order), so the clause means 'see how Muhammad is maimed.'
segments.inferno-28-g28: 'Dinanzi a me' = 'in front of me/before me'; 'sen va' is the contracted form of 'se ne va' meaning 'goes away' or here 'passes by' (reflexive + particle 'ne'); 'piangendo' is a gerund 'weeping' describing the manner; the proper name 'Alì' comes at the end for emphasis.
segments.inferno-28-g29: 'Fesso nel volto' = 'split in the face' (fesso = cleft/split) specifying the location with 'nel volto' (in the face). The phrase 'dal mento al ciuffetto' uses 'dal ... al ...' to mark the extent/range: 'from the chin to the little tuft/forelock.' The adjective 'fesso' precedes the noun as a descriptive state.
segments.inferno-28-g1: Chi poria mai pur con parole sciolte dicer del sangue e de le piaghe a pieno ch’i’ ora vidi,: "Chi" = interrogative 'who'. "poria" is a conditional form meaning 'could' (poetic/archaic conditional of poter/porre). "mai pur" = 'ever, even' (mai = ever; pur = even/still). "con parole sciolte" = a prepositional phrase 'with fluent/unrestrained words' (parole + adjective sciolte). "dicer" is the infinitive 'to tell/say' that governs the direct object introduced by "del/di"; "a pieno" here adverbially means 'fully' or 'completely'. "ch’i’ ora vidi" = relative clause 'that I now saw' (ch’ = che 'that'; i' = io 'I' in a poetic spelling; vidi = simple past 'I saw').
segments.inferno-28-g2: per narrar più volte?: "per" + infinitive expresses purpose 'in order to'. "narrar" is the infinitive 'to narrate/tell'. "più volte" = 'many times' or 'more than once' (più = more; volte = times). The whole phrase asks whether anyone could tell these things enough times to satisfy the need to recount them repeatedly.
segments.inferno-28-g3: Ogne lingua per certo verria meno per lo nostro sermone e per la mente c’hanno a tanto comprender poco seno.: "Ogne lingua" = 'every tongue' (every language/speech). "per certo" = 'certainly' or 'for a certainty'. "verria meno" is a conditional form meaning 'would fail/come up short' (verria = would come/fail). "per lo nostro sermone" = 'because of/through our discourse/speech' (sermone = speech). "e per la mente c’hanno" = 'and because of the minds they have' (c’ = ci/they have relating to us or to them; hanno = they have). "a tanto comprender poco seno" = literally 'to such an extent of understanding little bosom' — idiomatically 'have so little capacity to understand so much' (seno = figurative 'capacity/heart/breast').
segments.inferno-28-g4: S’el s’aunasse ancor tutta la gente che già,: The protasis begins with a conditional 'S’' = 'Se' (if). "s’aunasse" is a subjunctive/conditional form of the verb 'aunare' (to assemble/gather) with the reflexive pronoun "si" (s’), meaning 'were to gather themselves' or 'were gathered again'. "ancor" = 'again/still'. "tutta la gente che già" = 'all the people who formerly/once'. Grammatically this starts a long hypothetical clause introduced by 'if'.
segments.inferno-28-g5: in su la fortunata terra di Puglia,: "in su" is a poetic locative 'on/upon'. "la fortunata terra di Puglia" = 'the fortunate (blessed) land of Apulia' (terra = land, Puglia = Apulia). The whole prepositional phrase situates the people geographically.
segments.inferno-28-g6: fu del suo sangue dolente per li Troiani e per la lunga guerra che de l’anella fé sì alte spoglie,: "fu" = past indicative 'was'. "del suo sangue dolente" = 'sorrowful for its/their blood' (dolente = sorrowful). "per li Troiani e per la lunga guerra" = 'on account of the Trojans and the long war'. "che de l’anella fé sì alte spoglie" = relative clause 'which concerning the ring made such high/lofty spoils' (fé = fece 'made/caused'), referring to the Trojan conflict that produced great spoils.
segments.inferno-28-g7: come Livïo scrive,: Parenthetical relative 'as Livy writes'. "come" = 'as', "Livïo" proper name, "scrive" = present indicative 'writes' (authoritative citation).
segments.inferno-28-g8: che non erra,: A short relative clause modifying Livy: "che" = 'who/which', "non erra" = 'does not err' (present indicative), asserting Livy's reliability.
segments.inferno-28-g9: con quella che sentio di colpi doglie per contastare a Ruberto Guiscardo;: "con quella" = 'together with that one' (quella = that person). "che sentio" = 'whom I felt' or 'whose I felt' (sentio = I felt/heard — here 'I felt' or 'I perceived' the pains). "di colpi doglie" = 'pains from blows' (colpi = blows; doglie = pains). "per contastare a Ruberto Guiscardo" = 'when opposing Robert Guiscard' (contastare a = to contend/oppose). The clause adds an individual I personally witnessed suffering blows.
segments.inferno-28-g10: e l’altra il cui ossame ancor s’accoglie a Ceperan,: "e l’altra" = 'and the other'. "il cui ossame ancor s’accoglie" = 'whose bones still lie/are contained' (ossame = mass of bones; s'accoglie = is received/contained/lies). "a Ceperan" = 'at Ceprano' (place name).
segments.inferno-28-g11: là dove fu bugiardo ciascun Pugliese,: "là dove" = 'there where'. "fu bugiardo" = 'was deceitful/false' (past 'proved false'). "ciascun Pugliese" = 'each/every Apulian'. The clause explains something about Ceprano as a place where Apulians were false.
segments.inferno-28-g12: e là da Tagliacozzo,: "e" = 'and'. "là da Tagliacozzo" = 'there at Tagliacozzo' (another place reference).
segments.inferno-28-g13: dove sanz’ arme vinse il vecchio Alardo;: "dove" = 'where'. "sanz’ arme" = 'without arms' (sanz’ = senza). "vinse" = past 'won/vanquished'. "il vecchio Alardo" = 'the old Alard' (proper name). The clause says Alard prevailed there even without weapons.
segments.inferno-28-g14: e qual forato suo membro e qual mozzo mostrasse,: "e qual... e qual... mostrasse" = 'and whichever showed... and whichever showed...' "forato" = 'pierced' (past participle); "suo membro" = 'his member/limb' (membro can be limb or sexual member; context implies obscene mutilation). "mozzo" = 'cut off/severed'. "mostrasse" is imperfect subjunctive used in this hypothetical chain (would show), matching the conditional/hypothetical tone.
segments.inferno-28-g15: d’aequar sarebbe nulla il modo de la nona bolgia sozzo.: "d’aequar" = infinitive 'to equal/compare' (di aequar). "sarebbe" = conditional 'would be'. "nulla" = 'nothing' (i.e., there would be no way). "il modo de la nona bolgia sozzo" = 'the manner/mode of the filthy ninth bolgia'. Together: 'there would be no way to compare/put on a par the manner of the filthy ninth bolgia.'
segments.inferno-28-g16: Già veggia,: "Già" = 'already/now' (adverb). "veggia" is a poetic present form of 'vedere' meaning 'I see' or 'I perceive' (here used by the narrator to introduce what he is witnessing). The two together act as a present-tense exclamation 'Now I see'.
segments.inferno-28-g17: per mezzul perdere o lulla,: "per" = 'through/by/for'. "mezzul" (mezzule) = a stave of a cask; "perdere" = 'to lose' (infinitive); "o lulla" = 'or bottom' (lulla = cask bottom). The phrase is simile: 'as if a stave or the bottom of a cask had been lost', a visual comparison.
segments.inferno-28-g18: com’ io vidi un,: "com’" = contraction of "come" = 'as'/'just as'. "io vidi un" = 'I saw one' (io = I; vidi = past 'I saw'; un = one/a). This begins a relative/example clause introduced by the narrator's eyewitness report.
segments.inferno-28-g19: così non si pertugia,: "così" = 'thus/so'. "non si pertugia" = negative + reflexive of 'pertugiare' (to perforate/pierce) — 'it is not thus pierced' or 'it does not get perforated so'. "si" is the impersonal/reflexive pronoun forming a passive/impersonal construction.
segments.inferno-28-g20: rotto dal mento infin dove si trulla.: "rotto" = past participle 'broken'. "dal mento" = 'from the chin' (dal = da + il). "infin dove" = 'as far as where/up to the place where'. "si trulla" = reflexive present of 'trullare' meaning 'one breaks wind' (trulla = breaks wind/farts). The clause describes the head/face broken upwards as far as the place where the person farts, using the bodily and coarse imagery typical of the canto.
segments.inferno-28-g100: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking this clause to what precedes. Un ch’avea is 'a (man) who had' where ch’ is the elided relative pronoun che; avea is the imperfect (literary/archaic form of aveva) indicating a past state ('had'). L’una e l’altra man mozza literally lists 'the one and the other hand cut off' — l’una e l’altra = 'one and the other' (definite articles used for the two items), man = 'hand', and mozza is an adjectival past-participle 'cut off' placed after the noun.
segments.inferno-28-g101: Levando is a present gerund (gerundio) of levare, used here to describe an action simultaneous with the main verb ('raising' / 'while raising'). I is the plural definite article for moncherin (diminutive/plural of moncheri, 'stumps'). Per l’aura fosca = 'through/in the dark air' where per indicates motion/placement through or in, aura = 'air/breeze' and fosca is the adjective 'dark/gloomy' following the noun.
segments.inferno-28-g102: Sì che introduces a result clause ('so that'). Sangue is the grammatical subject ('the blood'), facea is the imperfect of fare used causatively ('was making/caused'), and la faccia sozza is the direct object phrase ('the face filthy') with the adjective sozza after the noun.
segments.inferno-28-g103: Gridò is the passato remoto (simple past) of gridare, 3rd person singular 'cried' or 'shouted', and it introduces the quoted speech that follows.
segments.inferno-28-g104: Ricordera’ti is an archaic/poetic form containing the verb ricordare with an attached clitic ti; contextually it functions as an address meaning 'remember (to you)' — effectively 'remember' directed at the listener. Anche means 'also'. Del = di + il 'of the' (here 'of'/'about'). Mosca is a proper name (not the insect) — the Florentine called Mosca.
segments.inferno-28-g105: Che is the relative pronoun 'who/that' referring back to Mosca; disse is the passato remoto (simple past) of dire, 'said'. This begins the relative clause identifying Mosca by what he said.
segments.inferno-28-g106: Lasso is an interjection meaning 'alas' or 'woe' (an archaic exclamation of grief or pity) placed outside the following quotation.
segments.inferno-28-g107: This segment is punctuation (a comma) marking continuation inside the reported speech; it has no lexical content to analyze.
segments.inferno-28-g108: The quoted phrase Capo ha cosa fatta is a simple clause: Capo (subject 'head' or 'leader'), ha (present indicative 'has'), and the direct object noun phrase cosa fatta ('a thing done' / 'the deed accomplished') where fatta is the past participle used adjectivally after cosa.
segments.inferno-28-g109: Che introduces an explanatory relative clause 'which'; fu is the passato remoto of essere 'was'; mal seme is a predicative noun phrase 'bad seed' (metaphoric). Per la gente tosca = 'for the Tuscan people' with per = 'for' and tosca qualifying gente as 'Tuscan'.
segments.inferno-28-g110: 'E io' is simply 'And I'; 'li' is a clitic pronoun referring back to those present (here 'to them' or 'to them/that company') and functions as the indirect object of 'aggiunsi'. 'aggiunsi' is first-person passato remoto of 'aggiungere' meaning 'I added' or 'I added/summed up (and said)'; it introduces the direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-28-g111: The quoted phrase 'E morte di tua schiatta' is an exclamatory curse: 'E' ('And' or 'So'), 'morte' nominally 'death' used vocatively/exclamatorily, 'di tua schiatta' 'to your stock/lineage' (di + possessive + noun). The phrase functions as a terse denunciation addressed within the narrative speech.
segments.inferno-28-g112: 'per' here introduces a consequence/cause roughly 'whereupon' or 'so that', 'ch’' is 'che', and 'elli' is the subject pronoun 'they'/'he' (archaic third-person pronoun) referring back to the person who reacted; together they begin the clause describing his reaction.
segments.inferno-28-g113: 'accumulando' is a present participle ('accumulating' or 'piling up') describing a concurrent action; 'duol con duolo' is an idiomatic repetition meaning 'grief upon grief' (literally 'pain with pain'), where the first 'duol' functions as the thing being accumulated and the second is the repetition for emphasis.
segments.inferno-28-g114: 'sen gio' is an archaic combination meaning 'se ne giò' or 'se ne andò' — 'he went away' (with 'sen' = reflexive/clitic 'se ne' meaning 'away from there' and 'gio' = 'giù'/'down' used adverbially); 'come' = 'like/as', 'persona' = 'person', and 'trista e matta' are two adjectival predicates 'sad and mad'. The whole phrase describes his departing appearance 'he went away like a sad and mad person.'
segments.inferno-28-g115: Ma is a simple adversative conjunction ('but'). rimasi is the passato remoto (1st person singular) of rimanere, meaning 'I remained/stayed'. The sequence a + infinitive (a riguardar) is a common older/poetic construction meaning 'to (be) looking at'; lo stuolo is the direct object 'the host/throng'.
segments.inferno-28-g116: e vidi = 'and I saw' (vidi is passato remoto, 1st sing. or 1st sing. form used narratively). cosa che io avrei paura is a subordinate clause introduced by che (= 'that') with avrei (present conditional of avere) meaning 'I would have' (i.e. 'I would feel') followed by paura 'fear'.
segments.inferno-28-g117: sanza is an archaic form of senza, meaning 'without'. più here means 'any more/any further' (i.e. 'no further'), and prova is used as a noun 'proof' or 'evidence' (poetic diction). The whole phrase means 'without further proof'.
segments.inferno-28-g118: di before an infinitive (contarla) introduces what is lacking: 'without further proof to recount it alone.' contarla is the infinitive with the clitic object -la 'it' (the thing seen); solo modifies contarla = 'to recount it alone' or 'to relate it by myself'.
segments.inferno-28-g119: se non che is an idiomatic concessive conjunction meaning 'except that' or 'unless that'. coscïenza (coscienza) is a noun 'conscience' or 'awareness'; m' is the clitic for mi 'me', and assicura is present indicative (3rd sing.) of assicurare 'assures' — 'except that conscience assures me'.
segments.inferno-28-g120: la buona compagnia is the feminine noun phrase 'the good company/group'. che introduces a relative clause; l’uom (uom = uomo) is 'man' and francheggia is present indicative (3rd sing.) of francheggiare — here roughly 'sets free/exempts/claims' (Dante's sense: that a man excuses or vindicates himself). sotto + l' + asbergo = 'under the shelter/shield' and del sentirsi pura is a noun phrase 'of the feeling oneself pure' (sentirsi used as a noun/idea); pura (feminine) agrees with compagnia or with the abstract notion expressed by the phrase.
segments.inferno-28-g30: 'E' is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. 'Tutti li altri' is a noun phrase 'all the others' with the archaic article 'li' before 'altri'. 'Che tu vedi qui' is a relative clause introduced by 'che' ('who/which/that'), with 'tu' as subject and 'vedi' in the present indicative (2nd person singular); 'qui' is a local adverb 'here'.
segments.inferno-28-g31: 'Seminator di scandalo e di scisma' is an appositive noun phrase describing those 'others' as 'sowers (instigators) of scandal and of schism' (two genitives joined by 'e'). 'Fuor vivi' is an archaic locution: 'fuor' (older form of 'fuori') + 'vivi' ('alive'), here meaning 'in life / when they were alive' (i.e. they sowed scandal while living).
segments.inferno-28-g32: 'E però' links what follows as a consequence ('and therefore' / 'and so'). 'Son' is an archaic short form of 'sono' (3rd person plural present of 'essere'). 'Fessi' is a past participle used adjectivally meaning 'split open' (not 'foolish' here). 'Così' is the adverb 'thus/like this' describing the state they are in.
segments.inferno-28-g33: Un diavolo è qua dietro = 'a devil is here behind (us/there)'; 'qua dietro' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'just behind'. 'Che' introduces a relative clause ('who'). 'N’' is an enclitic pronoun (archaic/poetic 'ne'/'ci') attached to the verb; here it marks the object affected ('us' / 'of it') in the phrase 'n'accisma'. 'Accisma' is an archaic/poetic verb meaning 'mangles/rends' and is used in the clause with the adverb 'sì crudelmente' ('so cruelly').
segments.inferno-28-g34: 'Al taglio de la spada' is a circumstantial phrase 'at the cut / stroke of the sword' (de = archaic 'di'). 'Rimettendo' is the gerund of 'rimettere' meaning 'putting back / restoring' and expresses an action concomitant with the sword's cut. 'Ciascun di questa risma' = 'each one of this company/band' (ciascun = each, risma = group/company in this context).
segments.inferno-28-g35: 'Quand’ avem volta' is the contracted form of 'quando abbiamo volta' — a periphrastic perfect meaning 'when we have turned'. 'Volta' here functions as the past participle/verb meaning 'turned' (from volgere). 'La dolente strada' = 'the doleful / sorrowful path'; the whole clause sets the temporal point when the devil restores each one.
segments.inferno-28-g36: 'Però che' here introduces the reason ('for / because'), followed by 'le ferite son richiuse' — 'the wounds are closed up again', with 'son' = 'sono' and 'richiuse' = past participle 'closed again'. 'Prima ch’ altri dinanzi li rivada' = 'before another comes back in front of them'; 'prima che' requires a subjunctive in archaic Italian, and 'rivad a' (rivadare) is used here as 'come back / meet again' — 'li' is an enclitic pronoun referring back to the persons/wounds.
segments.inferno-28-g37: 'Ma' = 'but'. 'Tu chi se’' is an emphatic word order for 'who are you' (with 'se’' an archaic form of 'sei', 'you are'). 'Che' introduces the relative clause. ''N su lo scoglio' is a locative phrase 'on the rock up there' (''n' = contracted 'in' or 'su' dialectal use). 'Muse' is an archaic/poetic verb/form (from the root 'muso' in Dante's usage) meaning roughly 'sit sulking/perch' or 'to be stationed there with a face/position' — here it functions as the verb of the relative clause ('who sit/perch').
segments.inferno-28-g38: 'Forse' = 'perhaps'. 'Per indugiar d’ire' is 'to delay of going' → 'to delay going' (indugiar = infinitive 'to delay'; 'd’ire' = 'di ire', 'of going' / 'to go' — 'ire' here = 'to go'). 'A la pena ch’è giudicata in su le tue accuse' = 'to the punishment that has been judged/declared upon your accusations'; 'ch’ è giudicata' is a relative clause with 'giudicata' as past participle used adjectivally ('decided/condemned'). 'In su' = 'upon/on', and 'le tue accuse' = 'your accusations'.
segments.inferno-28-g39: This segment contains only a punctuation mark (a period) and has no lexical material to analyze; it simply ends the preceding line.
segments.inferno-28-g121: 'Io vidi certo' is a simple past statement: 'Io' (subject) + passato remoto 'vidi' (I saw). 'certo' is an adverb after the verb meaning 'certainly' or 'for certain', reinforcing the assertion.
segments.inferno-28-g122: The clause is concessive/attendant: 'e ancor par' = 'and still it seems'. 'par' (present 'seems') takes a subordinate clause introduced by 'ch’' (che = 'that'); 'io' is the subject of the subjunctive 'veggia' ('that I may/might see it'), and 'l' is the object pronoun 'it'. The subjunctive here expresses appearance rather than factuality.
segments.inferno-28-g123: This is a noun phrase with an infinitive used attributively: 'un busto sanza capo' (a torso without a head) followed by the infinitive 'andar' describing its motion (like an English participle). 'sì come andavan li altri' is a comparative clause ('just as the others were going'); 'andavan' is imperfect, agreeing with 'li altri'.
segments.inferno-28-g124: 'e 'l capo tronco tenea per le chiome' is coordinate to the previous clause: 'e' = and; '’l capo tronco' = the severed/truncated head (adjective after noun); 'tenea' is the imperfect of 'tenere' meaning 'he held'; 'per le chiome' uses 'per' to mark the means/instrument ('by the hair').
segments.inferno-28-g125: 'pesol' is a compact/poetic form meaning 'it dangled/was swinging' (third-person sense); it is followed by the instrument 'con mano' ('with the hand') and the simile 'a guisa di lanterna' ('in the manner of a lantern'). Word order places the manner after the instrument for emphasis.
segments.inferno-28-g126: 'e quel mirava noi e dicea' = 'and that one looked at us and was saying'. 'mirava' (imperfect of 'mirare') is transitive here with 'noi' as its object ('us'); 'dicea' is an imperfect durative 'was saying', preparing the direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-28-g127: Interjectional direct speech: 'Oh me!' is an exclamation of lament; no special grammar beyond exclamation.
segments.inferno-28-g128: Punctuation only (full stop). No lexical items to analyze.
segments.inferno-28-g129: 'Di sé facea a sé stesso lucerna' literal structure: 'Di sé' ('of/from himself') marks the material/source, 'facea' is the imperfect of 'fare' ('made'), and 'a sé stesso' is a dative/benefactive reflexive ('to himself'); 'lucerna' is the predicate noun placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.inferno-28-g130: 'ed eran due in uno e uno in due' uses the imperfect 'eran' (they were) with a paradoxical, chiasmic expression 'two in one and one in two'; the repetition and inversion are poetic and emphasize a mysterious duality.
segments.inferno-28-g131: 'com’ esser può' = 'how it can be' — contraction 'com’' for 'come' ('how'); 'esser' is the infinitive 'to be'; 'può' is the present of 'potere' ('can'). The order (infinitive before modal) is poetic but equivalent to 'può esser'.
segments.inferno-28-g132: 'quei sa che sì governa' — 'quei' is a demonstrative pronoun functioning as subject ('that one' = 'he who'); 'sa' is present 'knows'; 'che' introduces the subordinate clause 'sì governa' ('thus governs'), where 'sì' is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying 'governa'.
segments.inferno-28-g133: Quando = 'when' (temporal). diritto is used adverbially here ('right/straight to'); al piè del ponte = 'to the foot/base of the bridge'. fue is the archaic/passato remoto 3rd person singular of essere (here 'he came/was' at that place).
segments.inferno-28-g134: levò is passato remoto (3rd sing.) of levar(e) 'he lifted/raised'. ’l is the contracted article 'the' before braccio 'arm'. alto 'high' qualifies the gesture, con tutta la testa = 'with his whole head' (a vivid poetic image). per + infinitive (per appressarne) introduces purpose ('in order to bring nearer'); appressarne is appressare + enclitic ne ('bring them near' — the words), and le parole sue 'his words' is the object.
segments.inferno-28-g135: che is a relative pronoun 'which' introducing the report of what he said; fuoro is the archaic passato remoto 3rd plural of essere (modern furono) 'they were' (i.e. his words were:).
segments.inferno-28-g136: Or = 'now' or 'at this moment'. vedi here is the 2nd person singular imperative or exhortation 'see!/look!' addressing the listener. la pena molesta = 'the troublesome/grievous punishment/suffering.'
segments.inferno-28-g137: tu che = 'you who...' — a direct vocative address followed by a relative clause marker che.
segments.inferno-28-g138: spirando is a present participle/gerund of spirare 'breathing' and here conveys 'while breathing' or 'alive and breathing' (Dante uses it to contrast the living who behold the dead).
segments.inferno-28-g139: vai is the present indicative (2nd singular) of andare 'you go'; veggendo is the present participle of veggĕre/vedere 'beholding', so vai veggendo = 'you go on beholding' or 'as you go you behold'. i morti = 'the dead' (direct object).
segments.inferno-28-g140: vedi again addresses the addressee ('see/consider'); s’ is a contraction of se (= 'if' / 'whether') here introducing an indirect question; alcuna means 'any (one)' or 'any' in the sense 'any person'; è is present indicative 'is'; grande come questa = 'as great as this.'
segments.inferno-28-g40: Né is the negative conjunction 'nor' used to continue a negation; 'morte' here refers to 'death' (used as a noun) and is the patient/force that might 'come' upon someone; 'giunse' is a past perfective verb (past tense 'came' / 'had come') and 'ancor' means 'yet' or 'still'. Together the clause says that death had not yet come upon him.
segments.inferno-28-g41: Né again continues the negation ('nor'); 'colpa' in classical usage would mean 'guilt' or 'fault' as the cause, and '’l' is the clitic for 'him/it'; 'mena' is a verb form meaning 'leads' or 'drives' (from menare). In this text the sense is 'nor does guilt drive him (to punishment)'. (The lexical sense list supplied does not include the precise meaning 'guilt' or 'lead', so those specific senses are marked as unknown.)
segments.inferno-28-g42: ‘Rispuose’ is the passato remoto (past tense) 'answered' (3rd person singular) and the following words '’l mio maestro' are the nominative phrase 'my master' (the article clitic 'l' + adjective 'mio' + noun 'maestro'), identifying the speaker.
segments.inferno-28-g43: 'a' is the infinitival/complement marker 'to' or 'in order to' and 'tormentarlo' is the infinitive 'tormentare' plus the enclitic object pronoun 'lo' (him/it): 'to torment him'. The clause marks purpose (what would be done) or the action that is not actually happening.
segments.inferno-28-g44: 'ma' is the adversative conjunction 'but'; 'per' here is purposive 'in order to'; 'dar' is the infinitive 'to give'; 'lui' is the indirect object/clitic meaning 'him'; 'esperïenza' is the noun 'experience' and 'piena' is the adjective 'full' modifying it. The phrase means 'but in order to give him full experience.'
segments.inferno-28-g45: 'a me' is the dative 'to me' (or 'for me'), indicating who must carry out the action; the preposition 'a' + pronoun 'me' point to the speaker as the one obliged to act.
segments.inferno-28-g46: 'che' introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; 'morto' is the adjective 'dead' describing the speaker; 'son' is the present tense of 'essere' (here 'am' for first person). The clause 'che morto son' means 'who am dead.' (The sense list given for 'morto' did not include the adjectival 'dead', so that sense is marked unknown.)
segments.inferno-28-g47: 'Convien' is an impersonal present tense meaning 'it is necessary' or 'one must'; 'menarlo' = 'menare' (to lead) + enclitic 'lo' (him) meaning 'lead him'; 'per lo ’nferno' = 'through Hell' (per + article + noun); 'qua giù' is an adverbial phrase 'here below'; 'di giro in giro' is a repeated expression 'from circle to circle' or 'from turn to turn'—literally 'from round to round'—indicating movement through successive circles. Together: 'it is necessary to lead him through Hell here below, from circle to circle.'
segments.inferno-28-g48: 'e' = 'and'; 'quest’ è ver' = 'this is true' (questo + essere + verità); 'così com’ io ti parlo' = 'just as I tell you' or 'as I speak to you' — 'così' = 'thus', 'com’' = 'as', 'io' = subject 'I', 'ti' = indirect object 'to you', 'parlo' = present 'I speak/tell'. This closes the quoted assurance that the statement is true.
segments.inferno-28-g49: 'Più fuor di cento' is an idiomatic numerical quantifier meaning 'more than a hundred' (literally 'more outside of a hundred'); 'che' introduces the relative clause that follows ('who'). The word order places the quantifier before the relative marker, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.inferno-28-g50: 'quando' introduces a temporal clause 'when'; 'l’udiro' is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'they heard it' (the clitic 'l'' = 'it' and 'udiro' corresponds to a past hearing form). Grammatically this clause gives the time at which the following action happened.
segments.inferno-28-g51: The reflexive clitic 's’' (si) attaches to 'arrestaron' (passato remoto, 3rd pl.) to mean 'they stopped' (literally 'they arrested themselves' = 'they halted'); 'nel fosso' = 'in the ditch/pit'; 'a riguardarmi' is an infinitive of purpose 'to look at me' where the object pronoun 'mi' is enclitic to the infinitive; 'per maraviglia' gives the reason 'out of/for wonder'.
segments.inferno-28-g52: 'oblïando' is the present participle (or gerund-equivalent) 'forgetting' showing a concurrent action; 'il martiro' = 'the torment' (martyrdom/torment) is its object. The phrase means 'forgetting the torment.'
segments.inferno-28-g53: Or is an adverb meaning 'now' (a frequent vocative/temporal opener in Dante). Dì is the imperative of dire here meaning 'tell' (direct command). The sequence a fra Dolcin is the indirect object: a + title fra + proper name (fra = brother/monk; note the word list did not include this sense). Dunque is a discourse adverb 'then/therefore' linking to the request; che introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. s’armi is si + armi (reflexive si contracted) + present subjunctive/imperative-like form armi = 'arm himself' (separate reflexive command).
segments.inferno-28-g54: Tu introduces the addressee 'you'. Che is the relative/temporal 'who/that' referring to tu. Forse 'perhaps' modifies the following verb. Vedra’ is the future indicative (vedrà) with the apostrophe marking the truncated vowel; it means 'will see'. Il sole 'the sun' is the object; in breve is an adverbial phrase 'in short/a short time' = 'soon'.
segments.inferno-28-g55: s’ello is the archaic contraction of se + ello (if he/it); non vuol is present indicative of volere 'does not want'. Qui tosto 'here at once' (qui = here/at this point; tosto = immediately/quickly). Seguitarmi is the infinitive seguire + enclitic object pronoun -mi 'to follow me' (the verb appears with the enclitic attached).
segments.inferno-28-g56: Sì di vivanda: sì here means 'so/such' (indicating degree) and di introduces the kind/quantity: 'of provisions/food'. The phrase is elliptical and sets up a result clause (so much food that...).
segments.inferno-28-g57: Che begins a result/relative clause ('so ... that ...'). Stretta is an adjective/past participle here from stringere meaning 'pressed/tightly packed' (used attributively with vivanda). Di neve = 'in/with snow' (shows how the food is packed). Non rechi is the present subjunctive of recare used in a result clause after sì...che ('so ... that ... may not bring'); la vittoria al Noarese = 'victory to the man of Novara' (al = a + il, Noarese = person from Novara).
segments.inferno-28-g58: Ch’ is the clitic form of che 'for/that' introducing the reason/result clause. Altrimenti 'otherwise'. Acquistar is the infinitive 'to acquire/gain' used impersonally; non saria is the conditional (archaic saria = sarebbe) 'would not be'. Leve (archaic spelling of lieve) means 'easy/light' — so 'would not be easy to gain'.
segments.inferno-28-g59: Poi che introduces a temporal clause 'after/when'. L’un piè = l' + un piè 'one foot' (subject). Per girsene is per + girsene: girsene is an old/enclitic form (girare + si + ne) meaning 'to turn oneself away/go off from here'; per indicates purpose or motion ('about to/for turning away'). Sospese is passato remoto (or poetic past) 'suspended/paused' agreeing with l’un piè.
segments.inferno-28-g60: Mäometto is the subject (Muhammad). Mi is the indirect object 'to me'. Disse is passato remoto of dire 'said/told'. Esta parola = 'this word' (esta is an archaic form of questa). The whole clause is 'Muhammad said this word to me.'
segments.inferno-28-g61: Indi is 'then/next' (a temporal adverb). A partirsi is a + infinitive partirsi (reflexive 'to depart') and expresses purpose here: 'to make [him] depart'. In terra 'on the ground' (adverbial). Lo is the direct object pronoun 'him'. Distese is passato remoto of distendere 'stretched/laid out'. Grammatically: 'then, to set him off, he laid him on the ground.'
segments.inferno-28-g141: 'E' is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; 'perché' here functions causally ('since/because'); 'tu' is the 2nd-person subject; 'di me' means 'about me' (di + pronoun); 'novella' is the direct object 'news'; 'porti' is 2nd-person singular present of portare (here meaning 'you bring/carry'), forming a subordinate clause giving the reason for the following imperative 'sappi'.
segments.inferno-28-g142: 'sappi' is the 2nd-person singular imperative of sapere ('know!'); 'ch’' is the elided form of 'che' introducing a subordinate clause ('that'); 'i’' represents the elided 'io' (here orthography shows clitic elision); 'son' is the archaic present 'sono' (1st-person singular 'I am'); 'Bertram dal Bornio' is the name plus a locative/epithet ('of Bornio'). The clause is an imperative address telling the listener to know the identity of the speaker.
segments.inferno-28-g143: 'quelli' picks out 'the one' (an identifier referring back to the named person); 'che' is a relative pronoun 'who/that'; 'diedi' is 1st-person singular passato remoto of dare ('I gave'); 'al re giovane' = 'to the young king' (a prepositional phrase: a + il → al); 'i ma’ conforti' is 'the bad/evil comforts/counsels' — 'i' is the plural article 'the', 'ma’' is an archaic/contracted form meaning 'mali' (evil), and 'conforti' is a plural noun 'comforts/counsels'. The relative clause further identifies Bertram by the harmful counsels he gave.
segments.inferno-28-g144: 'Io feci' is passato remoto 'I made/caused'; 'il padre e ’l figlio' = 'the father and the son' (note the contracted article '’l'); 'in sé' literally 'in themselves' meaning 'against one another' or 'mutually'; 'ribelli' is the predicate adjective 'rebellious' agreeing in plural. The verb causes a change of state: 'I made the father and son rebellious.'
segments.inferno-28-g145: Proper name 'Achitofèl' (Ahithophel) is the subject; 'non' negates the verb; 'fé' is passato remoto of fare ('did/made/caused'); 'più' here modifies the degree ('more' / 'any greater'); 'd’Absalone e di Davìd' is the indirect/object phrase introduced by 'di' (here meaning 'to/against Absalom and David'); 'coi' = con + i 'with the' introduces the instrument; 'malvagi' modifies 'punzelli' ('wicked'); 'punzelli' (literally 'goads' or 'spurs') is used metaphorically for incitements or prickings. The clause says Ahithophel did no greater deed in relation to Absalom and David than by his wicked spurs/incitements.
segments.inferno-28-g146: Perch' is the contracted poetic form of perché meaning 'because'. io is the subject pronoun 'I'. parti' here is an archaic/past form meaning 'I departed/left' (passato remoto or poetic past of partire); così giunte persone = 'people thus joined/assembled' (giunte modifying persone). The clause means 'because I departed from such assembled people.'
segments.inferno-28-g147: partito is a past participle used adjectivally 'divided/split' or 'having been parted'; porto is present indicative 1st person of portare 'I carry' (here 'I carry [it] divided'); il mio cerebro = 'my brain/mind' (cerebro is an archaic form of 'cervello'). The word order (partito porto) places the participle before the verb for emphasis.
segments.inferno-28-g148: lasso is an interjection/adjective meaning 'weary' or as an exclamation 'alas! / I am weary'. Here it is a brief emotional exclamation concluding the line.
segments.inferno-28-g149: A punctuation mark continuing the previous line; it separates clauses and has no lexical content to analyze.
segments.inferno-28-g150: 'Dal' is the contracted form of da + il, meaning 'from the' and indicating origin; it governs the noun phrase 'suo principio'. 'Suo' is a possessive adjective agreeing with the (masculine) noun 'principio' and here functions as 'its' (referring back to a previously mentioned masculine thing). 'Principio' is the head noun meaning 'beginning' or 'origin'. The relative clause begins with the elided 'ch’' (che) 'that/which' and the verb 'è' (present indicative of essere, 'is'), so 'ch’è in questo troncone' is a post‑nominal relative clause meaning 'which is in this stump'; 'in questo troncone' gives the location. Word order is standard: preposition + article (dal) + possessive + noun, followed by a relative clause introduced by che + verb + prepositional phrase.
segments.inferno-28-g62: Un altro is an indefinite nominal phrase functioning as the sentence subject: 'Un' is the indefinite article and 'altro' functions adjectivally/pronominally to mean 'another (man)'. The comma marks a pause before a long relative clause.
segments.inferno-28-g63: This is a relative clause introduced by che 'who/which' (relative pronoun). Forata (past participle used adjectivally) and tronco describe physical injuries; avea is the imperfect (he had) used descriptively. ’l is the contracted article (il) before naso; infin (short for infino) + sotto expresses extent 'as far as under'.
segments.inferno-28-g64: Non ... mai che is an idiomatic negative limitation: non ... mai = 'never' and non ... che = 'never more than / only'. Avea is again imperfect 'had'; sola is an adjective 'single/only' modifying orecchia.
segments.inferno-28-g65: Ristato is an archaic past participle/perfect form of ristare meaning 'he paused/ remained'; a + infinitive (a riguardar) marks purpose or the action he resumed: 'to look'. Per expresses cause ('out of/through wonder'). Con li altri = 'with the others'.
segments.inferno-28-g66: Innanzi a li altri = 'before/in front of the others' (prepositional phrase placing the action). Aprì is the remote past (passato remoto) 'he opened', and la canna is the direct object; canna here means 'throat/tube' used figuratively for mouth/throat.
segments.inferno-28-g67: Ch’era = che era 'which was' (relative + imperfect 'was') followed by di fuor d’ogne parte = 'on the outside on every side'; vermiglia is an adjective postposed for emphasis: 'vermilion/red all around'.
segments.inferno-28-g68: Simple narrative link: e 'and' plus disse, passato remoto of dire, introduces direct speech: 'and he said:'.
segments.inferno-28-g69: Direct address and two relative clauses: 'O tu' (vocative), cui colpa non condanna = 'whom/whose guilt does not condemn' (cui as relative pronoun, condanna present indicative/relative meaning 'condemn'), and 'cu’ io vidi su in terra latina' = 'whom I saw up on the Latin land' (cu’ = che/cu' 'whom', vidi = passato remoto 'I saw', su = 'up/on').
segments.inferno-28-g70: Conditional se 'if' introduces a parenthetical hedge. Troppa simiglianza is the subject 'too great a resemblance'; non m'inganna is a present indicative clause 'does not deceive me' with the clitic m' (mi) attached to the verb inganna and elided by apostrophe. Note m' is the clitic object 'me' placed before the verb.
segments.inferno-28-g71: Rimembriti is an imperative/hortative form meaning 'remember' (addressing the addressee). Di introduces the person remembered: 'Pier da Medicina' = Piero of Medicina (place-name). The personal name is a proper noun composed of given name + toponymic origin.
segments.inferno-28-g72: Se mai 'if ever' introduces a temporal conditional; torni is present (subjunctive or indicative form) used here in a future-in-conditional sense 'if you ever return'; a + infinitive veder = 'to see'; lo dolce piano = 'the sweet plain' (lo + adjective + noun); che introduces relative clause specifying the plain's slope 'that from Vercelli to Marcabò dichina.' Dichina is an archaic present form meaning 'slopes/descends' and thus functions as the verb of the relative clause.
segments.inferno-28-g73: E fa saper is a present-tense causative phrase 'and (he) causes it to be known' or more simply 'and tell.' A’ due miglior da Fano = 'to the two best/foremost (men) of Fano': miglior functions as a substantive adjective 'the best/foremost.'
segments.inferno-28-g74: a messer Guido e anco ad Angiolello: the indirect objects of fa saper. Messer is an honorific ('Messer, Sir'); anco = 'also'; ad indicates the indirect-object preposition before the second addressee.
segments.inferno-28-g75: Che here introduces the content clause (that-clause) reporting what should be made known—functioning like English 'that'.
segments.inferno-28-g76: Se ... non è vano = 'if ... is not vain.' L’antiveder is a substantive (a forewarning/foresight). Qui 'here' localizes the prediction; è is present 'is.'
segments.inferno-28-g77: Gittati saran is a future passive construction: 'they will be thrown' (gittati = past participle, saran = future of essere). Fuor di lor vasello = 'out of their little boat'; mazzerati is another past participle meaning 'drowned in sacks' (a specific medieval execution); presso a la Cattolica indicates location 'near the Cattolica.' Per introduces the motive/cause 'for/because of' tradimento d’un tiranno fello = 'the treachery of a wicked tyrant.'
segments.inferno-28-g78: Tra l’isola di Cipri e di Maiolica is a locative prepositional phrase 'between the island of Cipri and (that) of Maiolica.' Non vide mai = 'never saw' (vide passato remoto + mai 'ever/never'); sì gran fallo = 'so great a fault/crime'; Nettuno (Neptune) is placed after the object for emphasis.
segments.inferno-28-g79: Non da pirate is an ablative/agent phrase negated: 'not by pirates.' It reiterates who were not responsible for such a great crime.
segments.inferno-28-g80: Non da gente argolica continues the same negation pattern: 'not by Argolic (Argive/Greek) people.' Gente is a collective noun; argolica is adjectival (Argive/Greek).
segments.inferno-28-g81: Quel traditor = 'that traitor' (demonstrative + noun). Che vede pur con l’uno: che introduces a relative clause; vede is present 'sees'; pur 'even/still' intensifies; con l’uno = 'with the one (eye)'—the phrase describes the traitor's limited sight.
segments.inferno-28-g82: e tien la terra = 'and he holds/maintains the position' (tien = tener, present 'he holds' / 'maintains'); che introduces a subordinate content clause: 'that such a land here with me would wish to be without seeing'—vorrebbe is conditional 'would wish', di vedere is the infinitive complement, and esser digiuno is an idiom meaning 'to be lacking/without (experience of)'.
segments.inferno-28-g83: Farà venirli a parlamento seco is a causative-future: farà (he will make/see to it) + venirli (have them come; venire + clitic li 'them') to parlamento 'to a parley' with him (seco). The structure expresses 'he will summon them to parley with him.'
segments.inferno-28-g84: Poi farà sì: poi 'then' + farà 'he will do' + sì 'so/thus' — a short temporal/resultive transition: 'then he will do so.'
segments.inferno-28-g85: Ch’ introduces a result clause: ch’al vento di Focara non sarà lor mestier voto né preco. Al vento di Focara is a locative/idiomatic phrase ('by the wind of Focara'); non sarà = future negative 'there will not be'; lor mestier = 'their need' (mestier = necessity); voto né preco = 'vow nor prayer' (both nouns joined by né). Grammatically, two nouns (voto, preco) are coordinated as things they will not need.

sentences.inferno-28-s21: And I to him: “Show me and tell me — if you want me to bring up news of you — who that one with the bitter appearance is.”
sentences.inferno-28-s22: Then he put his hand on the jaw of one of his companions and opened his mouth for him, shouting: "This is the man himself — and do not speak."
sentences.inferno-28-s23: This one, driven out, overwhelmed doubt in Caesar, asserting that the provided man always suffered harm in waiting for him.
sentences.inferno-28-s24: O how terrified Curio seemed to me, with his tongue cut in his throat, who was so bold to speak!
sentences.inferno-28-s37: Thus the contrapasso is observed in me.
sentences.inferno-28-s5: Between his legs hung the entrails; the offal and the wretched sack that turns into shit whatever is swallowed seemed so.
sentences.inferno-28-s6: While I was intent on seeing everything about him, he looked at me and with his hands opened his breast, saying: "Now see how I tear myself apart!"
sentences.inferno-28-s8: Before me, Ali passes by weeping, his face split from the chin to the little forelock.
sentences.inferno-28-s1: Who could ever, even with fluent words, fully tell of the blood and of the wounds that I now saw, in order to recount them more than once?
sentences.inferno-28-s2: Every tongue would surely come up short on account of our speech and on account of the minds they have, which have so little capacity to grasp so much.
sentences.inferno-28-s3: If all the people who once, on the fortunate land of Apulia, were sorrowful for their blood because of the Trojans and because of the long war that over the ring made such lofty spoils — as Livy writes, who does not err — together with the one whose pains from blows I felt when he opposed Robert Guiscard; and the other whose bones still lie at Ceprano, where every Apulian proved false; and there at Tagliacozzo, where old Alard won without weapons; and whoever showed his member pierced and whoever showed it cut off — there would be no way to equal or match the mode of the filthy ninth bolgia.
sentences.inferno-28-s4: Already I see, as if a stave or a bottom of a cask were lost, just as I saw one — thus it is not pierced — broken from the chin up to where he breaks wind.
sentences.inferno-28-s25: And one who had both his hands cut off, raising his stumps in the dark air, so that the blood made his face filthy, cried: "Remember also Mosca, who said, alas!"
sentences.inferno-28-s26: "Capo ha cosa fatta," which proved a bad seed for the Tuscan people.
sentences.inferno-28-s27: And I added to them: 'And death to your lineage'; whereupon he, accumulating grief upon grief, went away like a sad and mad person.
sentences.inferno-28-s28: But I remained watching the throng, and I saw something that I would fear, without further proof to recount it alone; except that conscience assures me, the good company that a man frees himself with under the shelter of feeling himself pure.
sentences.inferno-28-s9: And all the others that you see here, sowers of scandal and schism when alive, are split open like this.
sentences.inferno-28-s10: There is a devil just behind us who mangles us so cruelly; at the sword's cut he restores each one of this company when we have turned the dolorous path; for the wounds are closed up again before another comes back to them.
sentences.inferno-28-s11: But who are you that sit on the rock up there, perhaps to delay going to the punishment that has been decreed on your accusations?
sentences.inferno-28-s29: I saw it for certain, and even now it seems that I see it: a torso without a head went on just as the others of the sad flock were going; and he held the severed head by the hair, swinging it with his hand like a lantern: and that one looked at us and kept saying, "Oh me!"
sentences.inferno-28-s31: He made himself a lamp to himself, and they were two in one and one in two; how that can be, he who thus governs knows.
sentences.inferno-28-s32: When he came straight to the foot of the bridge, he raised his arm on high with his whole head to bring his words nearer, which were: "Now see the grievous punishment, you who, breathing, go beholding the dead: see whether any is as great as this."
sentences.inferno-28-s13: “Nor had death yet come upon him, nor does guilt drive him,” my master answered, “to torment him; but in order to give him full experience, to me, who am dead, it is necessary to lead him through Hell here below, from circle to circle; and this is true just as I tell you.”
sentences.inferno-28-s14: More than a hundred of them, when they heard it, stopped in the ditch to look at me in wonder, forgetting the torment.
sentences.inferno-28-s15: Now tell Brother Dolcino, then, to arm himself — you who perhaps will soon see the sun — if he does not want to follow me here at once; and tell him to bring such provisions, packed in snow, that they will not bring victory to the man of Novara, for otherwise the conquest would not be easy to gain.
sentences.inferno-28-s16: When one foot paused as if to turn away, Muhammad said this word to me; then, to make him depart, he stretched him out on the ground.
sentences.inferno-28-s33: And since you bring news of me, know that I am Bertram of Bornio, the one who gave the young king those evil counsels.
sentences.inferno-28-s34: I made father and son mutually rebellious; Ahithophel did no more (in that matter) against Absalom and David with his wicked goads.
sentences.inferno-28-s35: Because I parted from among people so joined, I carry my brain divided, weary!
sentences.inferno-28-s36: , from its beginning which is in this stump.
sentences.inferno-28-s17: Another one, whose throat had been pierced and whose nose was cut off down beneath the eyelashes, and who had never more than a single ear, having stayed to look in wonder with the others, opened his throat before the others, which was vermilion all around, and said: “O you whom guilt does not condemn and whom I saw up on the Latin land, if too great a resemblance does not deceive me, remember Pier of Medicina, if you ever return to see the sweet plain that slopes from Vercelli to Marcabò.”
sentences.inferno-28-s18: And make it known to the two foremost men of Fano, to Messer Guido and also to Angiolello, that, if this forewarning here is not vain, they will be thrown out of their little boat and drowned in sacks near the Cattolica for the treachery of a wicked tyrant.
sentences.inferno-28-s19: Between the island of Cipri and that of Maiolica Neptune never saw so great a crime, not by pirates, nor by Argive people.
sentences.inferno-28-s20: That traitor who sees even with one (eye), and who holds that the land which here with me would wish to be without seeing such a thing, will make them come to a parley with him; then he will do so, that by the wind of Focara they will have no need of vow or prayer for them.
